mappings.rationaleAI: Core text addressing biogeographic distribution, regional floras and faunas, and the factors determining where organisms live (C, D).
Examines ecosystems, communities, biomes, producers/consumers/decomposers, and ecosystem productivity (A.2, A.3, B.5).
Explores coniferous, middle-latitude, and tropical rain forest biomes including their structure, diversity, and productivity (A.1.c).
Directly addresses the grassland and savanna biome including tropical vegetation, campo cerrado, and caatingas (A.1.c).
Directly addresses the desert biome including aridity, limiting factors, and desert-adapted organisms (A.1.c).
Explores ocean communities including benthos, plankton, nekton, adaptations to marine conditions, and marine productivity (B.2).
Examines the sea as a biological environment and marine ecosystems including open-sea, estuarine, and neritic zones (B.2.a, B.4).
Addresses lacustrine (standing-water) freshwater communities including lakes, ponds, and associated organisms (B.3.a).
Addresses riverine (flowing-water) freshwater communities including rivers, streams, and their biological communities (B.3.b).
Reef ecosystems represent a major neritic/boundary environment between water and land, with rich biodiversity and community structure (B.4.b).
Explores the tundra biome and Arctic ecosystems, a major specialized polar environment (A.1.c, A.1.d).
Examines polar biomes in the Southern Hemisphere as specialized terrestrial and marine ecosystems (A.1.d).
Altitude-based zonation of organisms and mountain ecosystems harbor distinct biotic communities (A.1.c, A.2.a).

mappings.vsiAuthor: David George Bowers, Emyr Martyn Roberts
mappings.rationaleAI: Tidal processes shape boundary ecosystems between water and land, including biological productivity in intertidal zones (B.4).
Climate is the primary determinant of biome distribution and the environmental gradients underlying biogeographic patterns (A.1, C).
Non-native species disrupt regional floras and faunas and reshape biogeographic distributions (C, D).
Glaciation profoundly shaped biogeographic distribution and determined present-day regional floras and faunas (C, D).
Plant diversity, growth habits, and seed plant distribution are relevant to terrestrial producers and indicator organisms (A.2).
